quote_of_day.name: Anon

articles.title: Weather on the board today
articles.body:
A low-pressure system of football enthusiasm moved over the board, bringing clear joy and idealism.

Anon expressed love for South American samba-style football, calling it the 'greatest joy on earth.' However, this system was met with a cold front of cynicism and derision, quickly dampening the initial euphoria.

A scattered high-pressure ridge of youthful boasts formed, with one anon claiming to be 21 in college. This drew some interest but remained a brief, isolated phenomenon.

Meanwhile, a complex low-pressure system of conspiracy theories and denials developed, featuring claims of ISI agents and anti-Semitic and casteist remarks. A disputed claim about an ISI agent sparked a downpour of hostile comments.

Elsewhere, a light drizzle of board gossip and suggestions to post elsewhere on the internet persisted, with one anon recommending /b/ to a fitness enthusiast.

articles.title: Football enthusiasm met with cynicism and derision
articles.body:
Anon expressed admiration for football, calling it the "greatest joy on earth," particularly when played in the "South American samba style." They wished India could also appreciate this joy.

Another anon countered that sports like football lead to "child abuse" and "permanent health issues and injuries" with little to show for it.

They claimed that football is "notorious" for this and compared it to the Olympics, calling both "low yield child abuse factories." A respondent challenged the idea that football is a valuable pursuit, stating that "most sports players never achieve anything except gaining permanent health issues and injuries." They also suggested that India should not allow football unless it can benefit financially from it.

One anon defended the pursuit of excellence in education, saying that cracking the JEE or MBBS exams is a better strategy than becoming a footballer.

They joked that they would rather "abuse" their child to succeed in these exams than have them become a footballer.

Another anon criticized those who prioritize education over sports, calling them "fat loser pajeets" who want to participate in football hype because they are "victims of Jewish slop." They also mentioned that former Olympic winners often end up "hurt physically for life, mentally scarred by the child abuse, and usually poor with no career." A respondent brought up the example of the US, claiming that "US WASPs" realized they had no leverage over football, which is ruled by FIFA, allegedly owned by Jews.

They suggested that Indian bureaucrats are "just as smart" as US WASPs.

The conversation also touched on the exploitation of children in various systems, with one anon stating that the JEE system is a form of exploitation, but a beneficial one that allows some to escape menial labor.

They joked that they would rather have their child focus on cracking the JEE than become a footballer.

The discussion remained critical of football as a valuable or safe pursuit, with several anons expressing disdain for the sport and its perceived consequences.

articles.title: Anon claims to be 21 in college
articles.body:
Anon started a thread saying they are 21 in college. Another anon responded with a seemingly unrelated comment about a Muslim book and making a first impression.

A respondent asked if there are jobs in a particular field, sharing they are BTech in the same field and struggling with empowerment.

Anon then mentioned having two years left to finish their degree, which their parents forced them to opt for. One participant joked about someone getting "mogged" by a random coder.

A different anon claimed to have a database of over 1000 "mulliyo" and shared an anecdote about a "cuckmulle" who got exposed. Anon also discussed organizing their workspace, with one suggesting it would improve productivity.

The conversation also touched on NEET exams, with one anon stating they never appeared for it and don't consider themselves smart enough to be a doctor.

Some anons exchanged comments about learning Python and AI/ML, with one asking what degree another had before pursuing AI/ML.

The thread included some profane and racist remarks, including one anon saying "Al-zutt in the butt" and another telling someone not to abuse Allah.

articles.title: Anon disputes ISI agent claim, sparks anti-Semitic and casteist remarks
articles.body:
Anon opened the thread by expressing skepticism towards another poster's claim that current events are caused by Jews, suggesting instead that the original poster might be an ISI agent spreading falsehoods.

Another anon responded by disagreeing that everything happening is due to Jews, but expressed concern about Jewish influence and the normalization of pornography.

This poster also made a remark about Allah and Muslims, which drew a response from another anon who corrected them on their phrasing and added a joke about Periyar.

The conversation quickly devolved into anti-Semitic and casteist remarks, with one anon labeling Jews as "kikes" and making a comment about their supposed influence.

Another poster referred to Muslims as "muzzies" and made a derogatory comment about their skin tone.

The thread also saw several personal attacks, with one anon telling another to "chodo chorahe pr saareaam" and another saying "teri maa ka bdsm karu tere janeu se". One poster also threatened to desecrate a Quran.

Despite some anons arguing that being wary of Jews is common sense, others described such views as "retarded".

The thread also saw several claims about the caste and background of other posters, with one anon accusing another of being a "pasmanda". Elite whites were mentioned as being more blameworthy than Jews by one poster.

The thread was marked by a significant amount of hostility and prejudice, with several anons exchanging insults and derogatory comments.

articles.title: Anon Suggests Fitness Poster Take to /b/
articles.body:
Anon urged a fitness enthusiast to post on /b/ instead of solely on /fit/, where they had a thread. about their workout routine. The original poster described /b/ as not being able to stay on topic and becoming a "shitfest" about certain subjects.

They contrasted this with the fitness board, where one user, possibly IAC, posts daily about their progress. Replies included speculation about the fitness poster's identity and their decision to post on /fit/.

Some defended the poster, describing them as dedicated and focused on self-improvement.

articles.title: Rainy nights and past loves
articles.body:
Anon described a recent outing with a friend on a rainy night, finding beauty in the yellow lights, coffee, and fog on glass.

Another anon shared a personal reflection on past relationships, mentioning a deep connection with someone met in their teenage years.

This anon claimed to be happy now, having moved on from a period of suffering.

A respondent expressed longing for someone they loved, who was far away, and noted that seeing happy couples reminded them of this person.

Anon also discussed the pain of memories and the danger of nostalgia, calling it a 'slow poison.'
